PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW 1. Purpose This Overview aims to provide the reader with sufficient information to understand the purpose of the NHS Grampian, the key risks to the achievement of its objectives and how it has performed during the year. Should you wish to find out more detailed information we have provided this in the rest of the Performance Report, the Accountability Report and the financial statements. NHS Grampian publishes a wide range of information on its services and activities on its website which you can find at: https://www.nhsgrampian.org/ 2. Statement from the Chief Executive Looking back on 2023/24, I am proud of our achievements, as a health and care system. This has been enabled by the continued support of our colleagues, partners and the public during a period of challenge for us all. I am extremely grateful for everything that people in communities across Grampian have done and continue to do to support each other and NHS Grampian and all those providing care services on our behalf. I would like to express my thanks and appreciation to all colleagues for their outstanding contributions and for their relentless professionalism. The care and compassion shown to people in our care and to colleagues has been exemplary, as we have addressed the enduring system pressures and other challenges we have faced. I would also wish to thank all our partners for their collaborative support – our care homes, local authorities, our national health services colleagues and local resilience partnership members. Our whole system co-ordination and collaboration, along with the strength of our local Health and Social Care Partnerships has helped us continue to deliver our vital services to the people of the North East of Scotland. Over the last 12 months we have progressed in delivering our Plan for the Future (2022- 2028). Together with our colleagues, partners and the public we are committed to planning for a better future and transforming our services in support of the delivery of safe, high quality, person-centred and sustainable health services over the next five years and beyond. System pressures continue to present a challenge and we are further redesigning, with partners our services to ensure that we can continue to meet the health and social care needs of our population and provide a safe environment for our staff and most vulnerable patients. Many of the changes that we have already made will provide a greater level of care in our communities and build on the excellent foundations established through the integration of health and social care.

PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW The financial challenges NHS Grampian faced in 2023/24 are highlighted in the Annual Report and Accounts for this year. The Accounts show a small underspend against the revenue budget, but this was only achieved due to additional repayable financial support (known as “brokerage”) being provided to the Board by the Scottish Government. This highlights the severe financial pressures currently being faced by the health system in the North East. This challenging financial position is anticipated to continue into future years. The Chief Executive Team of the Board have been working to implement a risk based approach to managing this challenge which involves achieving an appropriate balance between our financial position, clinical governance matters, staff wellbeing and prevention. During the year a significant amount of work has been undertaken to further develop and implement a local value and sustainability programme engaging widely with colleagues across the system. The purpose of this programme is to deliver short term efficiency savings and over the medium to longer term work on how we transform services and pathways of care to make them sustainable in terms of quality of care, staffing and finance. I recognise the significant financial and workforce challenges not only facing NHS Grampian but impacting on the whole public sector. We will continue to work with our partners, citizens, and colleagues to explore opportunities for greater collaboration locally, regionally and nationally to ensure provision of high quality, safe, efficient and sustainable services which meet the needs of the population. Our operational performance has been impacted by the health debt generated over the recent years of the COVID-19 pandemic and recovery and our low bed base. During the financial year 2023/24 we have not been able to balance our revenue budget and have relied on using one-off income, savings and additional funding to support the financial position. These income and savings will unfortunately not be available in future years, and I do not underestimate the challenge facing NHS Grampian in balancing future budgets. In 2023/24 NHS Grampian has also been escalated from Stage 1 to Stage 2 of the Scottish Government’s support & intervention framework because of the Board’s financial position. During this period of preparing for the future your input and support will be important, and I would thank you in advance for working with colleagues and services as we make the necessary changes over the coming months. I wish to thank my predecessor and it has been a privilege to be the Interim Chief Executive at this time and I am incredibly grateful for your support. I look forward to working with the Board, Chief Executive Team, our staff, and partners in addressing the challenges that we face, implementing our Plan for the Future and continuing to provide the quality of care that our key stakeholders and the people of Grampian expect from us. Interim Chief Executive

PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW 3. Background and Principal Activities Grampian Health Board is responsible for leading efforts to improve the health of the people in Grampian, and for providing the health care services that people need. We also provide some specialist clinical and support services to other NHS Boards within the North of Scotland and some national services for the whole of Scotland. 17,000 directly employed staff and a range of independent primary care practices (69 General Medical, 130 Pharmacy, 91 Dental and 54 Opticians) provide the full range of primary, community and specialist health services to the over half-million people who live in Grampian. Services are provided at over 100 locations and where possible in people’s own homes across an area covering 3,000 square miles of city, town and village and rural communities. We are the fourth largest health board in Scotland by general funding allocation. Services are delivered based on a portfolio management structure covering Family services, Surgical and Clinical Support Services, Medical and Unscheduled Care, Mental Health and wellbeing, Population Health and services across Aberdeen City, Aberdeenshire, and Moray. The portfolio arrangements include, a range of Primary and Community Care services delegated to the three Integration Joint Boards (IJBs) covering Aberdeen City, Aberdeenshire, and Moray. All our direct clinical services are supported by corporate functions such as Infrastructure and Sustainability, E-Health, Finance and People and Culture. The IJBs are legally constituted cross-sector bodies providing a focus for NHS Grampian to work in partnership with other agencies, including Local Authorities and Primary Healthcare contractors, in the provision of integrated services to the local communities. Executive Director and Non-Executive members of the NHS Grampian Board are members of the Integration Joint Boards which provide the strategic direction for the integration of adult health and social care.

PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW 4. Delivering Healthcare – Finding Balance NHS Grampian’s Delivery Plan which was adopted by the Board in August 2023. The Delivery Plan covering the period August 2023 to March 2026 has been developed jointly with our three Health and Social Care Partnerships, colleagues, citizens, and wider partners. The plan contained our objectives and key priorities. The agreed objectives over the period August 2023 to March 2026 were: 1. Strengthen colleague and citizen engagement to improve health (People) 2. Create the conditions for sustainable change (Places) 3. Improve preventative and timely access to care (Pathways) The priorities identified aim to make a significant impact in relation to stabilisation, including navigating winter well and create the conditions to deliver the significant changes required to achieve the ambitions set out in our strategy - ‘ Plan for the Future 2022-28 ’. NHS Grampian was unable to set a balanced budget for this financial year and is expected to be the case in the medium term. This is a consequence of the ongoing pressures experienced in the health and care system and the negative impact this is having on both those accessing and delivering services. The Board is focused on ‘Finding Balance’ supporting change across the system considering the three strands of governance (clinical, financial and people including workforce and citizens) whilst maintaining a focus on prevention. A number of measures to manage the high pressures experienced to stabilise and transform the health and care services are being progressed whilst balancing the need for fiscal control in both short- and longer-term delivery of care. Our priorities are framed around the key components of our strategy - People, Places and Pathways, which encompass the 10 Scottish Government recovery priorities which is illustrated below. (Source: Delivery Plan 2023-26) An Integrated Performance Assurance Framework has been developed recognising that performance management is critical to delivering on the shared objectives and priorities in the Plan for the Future.
NHS GRAMPIAN ANNUAL REPORT AND ACCOUNTS FOR YEAR ENDED 31 MARCH 2024 PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW 6

PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW Summary of Key Issues Affecting Overall Delivery - Seasonal increased high level of pressure across the system, impacting on the need to re-prioritise capacity, reduced ability to release time to engage has been a significant challenge. - System pressures continue to impact on hospital flow and delivery of Unscheduled Care standards which is leading to increased planned care backlog and waits, after previous good progress in some of these areas, despite much committed and hard work by our staff, as evidenced by delayed discharges, 4 hour Emergency Department waits and Treatment Time Guarantee waits, although cancer treatment times have largely been stable. - Ability to balance meeting agreed financial performance whilst delivering planned and urgent/unscheduled care standards whilst responding to sustained and unprecedented operational pressures across the system remains an ongoing risk. The ambitious saving targets in place for this financial year have been met, although the impact of these remains and continues to present challenges. - Workforce, finance, and system pressures in general continue as enduring risks affecting NHS Grampian’s ability to maintain momentum towards delivery of our identified strategic objectives. 5. Going Concern Assessment Grampian Health Board is a statutory body and was established in 1972 under the National Health Services (Scotland) Act 1972 and is responsible for commissioning services for the residents of Grampian. NHS Grampian submitted its 2023/2024 financial plan to the Scottish Government in March 2024. The plan does not represent a break-even position recognising the significant work to be undertaken to bring the Board back into sustainable financial balance. However, despite these financial performance challenges it is assumed the organisation has adequate support from the Scottish Government support to continue in operational existence for the foreseeable future. There are no material uncertainties relating to events or conditions to date that, individually or collectively, may cast significant doubt on the ability of the board to continue in its operation. The accounts have been prepared on a going concern basis, as the NHS Board will continue in operational existence and continue to provide services for the foreseeable future, with no indication by Scottish Ministers of any intention for dissolution.

PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW 6. Summary of Performance Appraisal The Board monitors its performance against a range of quality and performance measures and submits performance information to the Scottish Government and other bodies throughout the year. Accordingly, operational (non-financial) information is taken from live systems and is subject to revision at a future date.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 1. Delivering the Plan for the Future As outlined above, our approach to delivering the ambitions set out in the Plan for the Future is focussed on three pillars (People, Places and Pathways), along with key priorities highlighted by the Scottish Government. Our Annual Delivery Plan is our mechanism to set out the organisation’s priorities for change which the Board (via sub committees), and the Scottish Government seeks assurance on. The Delivery Plan acts as a contract between the NHS Board and the Scottish Government. A significant milestone in 2023-24 led to the consultation and endorsement of NHS Grampian’s Integrated Performance Assurance and Reporting Framework (IPARF) designed to create better alignment against the Plan for the Future and the Delivery Plan. The Framework describes the: - Principles for performance management and assurance - Values and behaviours underpinning the Framework, particularly the need to approach performance assurance with a spirit of enquiry and learning rather than to be punitive or seek to assign blame for under-performance. - Dual purpose of performance reporting within NHS Grampian; it is not only to provide assurance to the Board and Chief Executive Team whether targets are being met, it also helps internal teams regularly monitor and assess their progress, ensuring actions are effective and aligned with desired outcomes. This data-driven approach enables the identification of areas for improvement and helps justify the organisation's chosen priorities. - The provision of a tiered approach to performance reporting across NHS Grampian. Annual Delivery Plan 23/24: Overall Status of Q4 Milestones at 29th March 2024: Of the 101 Deliverables reported in Q4 Milestones set out in the Delivery Plan, by the end of March 2024, 30% are currently on track with 26% complete at the end of Quarter 4.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Summary of Key Risks/Issues Affecting Overall Delivery People: - 29% of 24 Deliverables reported in Q4 Milestones are on track, 37% complete and 13% reported as significant delay - Progress on areas such as o Colleague inclusivity o National pay reform o Recruitment o Putting People First - Areas significantly deviating from plan o Reduction in locum staff use o Development of values-based culture delays o Support for people managers Places: - 26% of 57 Deliverables reported in Q4 Milestones on track, 23% complete and 17% reported as significant delay - Progress on areas such as o Achieved savings target o GRACE & GEMINI projects o Health Equity Plan & Population Health approaches o Dr Gray’s Strategic Plan - Areas significantly deviating from plan: o Fleet CO 2 emissions o Baird and Anchor new build o Hospital@Home Pathways: - 40% of 20 Deliverables reported in Q4 Milestones on track, 20% complete and 15% reported as significant delay - Progress on areas such as o RACH capacity o Increased bed capacity o Child Oral Health & Vaccination - Areas significantly deviating from plan: o Delay in planned re-opening of Short Stay Theatre 1 o Dermatology & Endoscopy projects delayed due to funding issues

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 2. Non-Financial Performance Summary 2.1 Key National Treatment Targets Our performance against the key national treatment targets is summarised below: Attendance at Accident & Emergency 98% of patients should wait no more than 4 hours from arrival to admission, discharge or transfer for accident and emergency treatment. Performance - Total A&E attendances in March 2024 were 10,878, an increase of 11.4% over March 2023. - ED attendance rates per 100,000 population in NHS Grampian remain consistently among the lowest in Scotland. - The percentage seen within 4 hours in an A&E department in March was 67.8% (65.2% for March 2023) compared to the Scotland wide rate of 67.4% (68% March 2023). - The number who waited over 4 hours in March 2024 was 3,478 of whom 370 waited over 12 hours, compared to 3,396 (250 over 12 hours) in March 2023.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS The pressures on Emergency Departments (ED) during the year continue to be well publicised across the NHS in Scotland and the UK. Our local analysis suggests that the ED performance was acting as the bellwether of the pressures experienced right across the whole health and social care system with capacity and flow factors affecting each step in a patient’s pathway combining to manifest at the front door of the hospital affecting the 4-hour performance. Delayed Discharge Reduction in the number of patients awaiting discharge from hospital into a more appropriate care setting, once treatment is complete. Performance - There were 166 patients, of which 154 were more than 3 days delayed at the March 2024 census date (72 at March 2023, of which 70 were delayed more than 3 days) ▪ There was no additional funding from Scottish Government for winter 2023/24, unlike 2022/23 ▪ There have been improvements in recording delayed discharges during the year - Of the mainland Boards, NHS Grampian continues to have one of the lowest numbers of standard and code 9 delays per 100,000 population.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS - Patients spent 5,106 days in hospital due to delays in discharge in Grampian in March 2024 (2,575 in March 2023). Treatment Time Guarantee 12-week in-patient / daycase Treatment Time Guarantee (TTG) and 12 weeks for first outpatient appointment. Performance - The tables below set out the waiting list size for the in-patient/daycase (TTG) and new outpatient (consultant-led) appointment waiting lists at quarter end - The in-patient/daycase (TTG) list size has been trending down through the year, to the lowest level since September 2022 - The new outpatient appointment (consultant-led) waiting list size increased through the first half of 2023/24, to its highest level at the end of October; there was then a decrease over the next 3 months, with a small increase through March. - The proportion of patients waiting over 12 weeks for in-patient/daycase treatment was 77% at the end of 2023/24 (78.2% at the end of 2022/23) - 54.1% of patients on the new outpatient list were waiting over 12 weeks at the end of 2023/24 (49.2% at 31 March 2023)

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS - Grampian has an established clinical prioritisation system (Elective Surgery Category System (ESCatS)) for planned care. This ensures that all urgent and priority patients are identified following determination of treatment and that patients are seen according to clinical need. - Capacity building has been targeted at urgent and priority patients (ESCatS 0 and 1 including cancer) and to address long waiting patients. Nevertheless, the waiting list size for all ESCatS 0 and 1 has increased during 2023/24.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS - The number of patients waiting over 104 weeks has increased through the year, to 630 new outpatients (1.2% of list size) and 2,021 TTG patients (11.7% of list size) at the end of 2023/24; in comparison to 268 new outpatients (0.6% of list size) and 1,841 TTG patients (10.1% of list size) at the end of 2022/23. Cancer Cancer Access Times: 31 days from decision to treat (95%) and 62 days from urgent referral with suspicion of cancer (95%). Performance - Cancer performance against the 31-day standard has been below the 95% standard for each quarter of 2023/24. - The 62-day performance has decreased through 2023/24 and has not yet met the national standard. - An increased rate of both urgent suspected cancer (USC) referrals and backlog in Urology & Colorectal pathways continues to be seen in Grampian, mirrored by the overall national picture. A significant factor in the breach of patients is access to theatre capacity, which is also under pressure from the complications of long waiters presenting with more advanced stage of surgery as well as high acuity complex non-cancer surgery. - Performance against both targets is shown below.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Mental Health Services Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS): treatment within 18 weeks of referral (90%) Performance - CAMHS performance has been above the 90% target for 2023/24, with the exception of July and August 2023 - Completed waits within 18 weeks was 69.6% for July 2023. This was a decrease from 92.9% in June, due to these reports now including patients who had originally been filtered to the Neurodevelopmental (ND) Pilot, which has come to end. Any patients who had not yet been seen through the ND Pilot were being offered a ND Targeted Choice appointment to decrease waiting times for those entering the ND Pathway. Many of these patients were seen in the month of July.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Adult Psychological Therapies: treatment within 18 weeks of referral (90%) Performance - Psychological Therapies performance has not met the 90% target during 2023/24, however performance has been trending up over the period.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 3. Risk and Uncertainty To support the success and effectiveness of NHS Grampian’s service delivery and governance arrangements, an enterprise risk management approach is adopted, which seeks to uniformly identify, assess, and manage risk in a proactive manner. This approach is formalised within the NHS Grampian Risk Management Protocol. Below details risks contained within the Strategic Risk Register during 2023/2024, which outline the significant risk profile which is being managed as part of the delivery of the Board’s Objectives. Risks that are assessed as being outwith the organisations agreed tolerance levels are subject to enhanced monitoring and review.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS During 2023/2024 the Board continued to manage risk in a way that focused on the safe, effective and person-centred delivery of clinical services, and helped fulfil the key operational objectives set out in our Delivery Plan. The last year has also seen the development and implementation of the Board Committee Risk Process, which aligns the organisation’s most significant risk to the Chief Executive Team, the Board Committees, and the Board itself. The development of Strategic Risk within the Board continues, including the identification of new or emerging risks. The Board’s risk management processes are covered in more detail in the Governance Statement on Pages 56 to 58.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 3. Financial Performance and Position 3.1 Consolidated Accounts The Annual Accounts consolidate the results of Grampian Health Board (NHS Grampian), Grampian Health Board Endowment Funds (Operating as NHS Grampian Charity) (a registered Charity) and the three Integration Joint Boards (IJBs) (Aberdeen City, Aberdeenshire, and Moray). The basis of consolidation, explained in note 1 Accounting Policies on Page 97, is determined by the extent of control NHS Grampian can effectively exercise over each of its partner organisations. All Trustees of the NHS Grampian Charity are members of NHS Grampian. The Board therefore has majority control and, using the principles stipulated by IFRS 10, must consolidate, in full, the financial results of the NHS Grampian Charity into the main Board accounts. Voting membership of each of the IJBs is however split equally between members of NHS Grampian and members of each local council. The Board, therefore, has exactly half of the controlling interest in each IJB and, under IAS 28, is required to consolidate only this share of the net assets or net liabilities, as a Joint Venture, into the main Board accounts. PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS The charity’s investment strategy targets investments to deliver sustainable long-term capital growth and consistent income generation, and income generation has been maintained during 2023/24. The national and international economic and political environment was extremely volatile during 2022-23 leading to a reduction in the value of the investment portfolio of circa 7% (£3.7M). Market conditions have been more favourable during 2023-24 with the result that the previous reduction in value has been almost reversed with the portfolio growing in value by just over £3.0 million throughout the year to a current level of £52.3 million. The Charity relies upon the generosity of patients, their relatives, and friends, who have experienced the care of NHS Grampian, and other donors who are generous in their support of the Charity. PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS significant operational pressures during 2023/24 there was a continuing need to develop the model of delivery for many of the core services provided by each of the three IJB’s. All three IJB’s are continuing to experience underlying financial challenges, which reflect demographic and demand pressures resulting in higher numbers of people receiving support in the community. There continued to be a major focus on preventing admissions, supporting people in the community, and minimising the level of delayed discharges in Grampian hospitals. These efforts were underpinned by developments in digital innovations and the community engagement agenda across all three IJB’s. A key priority continues to be the development of whole system partnership arrangements to embed learning and transformational change into clinical strategy and core service provision moving forward. The IJBs faced operational pressures and due to the worsening financial position over the year, Moray and Aberdeenshire IJBs required an additional transfer of resource from NHS Grampian and their Council partner to achieve balance following depletion of IJB reserves. The level of IJB Reserves at the end of the 2023/24 has reduced significantly from those reported in 2022/23. This is due to the requirement by the Scottish Government that IJBs should use their earmarked Reserves to finance expenditure before receiving new funding allocations in 2023/24 and the requirement to apply Reserves to balance financial performance during 2023/24. The reported financial results for each of the IJBs is summarised below: 3.1.2.1 Aberdeen City IJB Aberdeen City IJB reported an overall deficit of £17.311 million for the year (2022/23 deficit £24.246 million), reducing the retained reserves carried forward to £9.835 million (2022/23 £27.146 million). This reflects: A general risk fund of £2.5 million (2022/23 £2.5 million) towards the impact of - unexpected events or emergencies; and Earmarked reserves of £7.334 million (2022/23 £24.646 million) due to the - allocation of funding to the IJB by the Scottish Government for a range of purposes including Mental Health Workers and Integration and Change. PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 3.2 NHS Grampian performance against statutory financial targets The results of each of the partner organisations, although consolidated with the main Board accounts for group accounting purposes, do not form part of the statutory financial target set for NHS Boards by the Scottish Government Health and Social Care Directorates (SGHSCD) and are, therefore, not taken into account when considering the Board’s in year financial performance. NHS Boards have the ability to manage small under/overspends (within 1% of resource) of an overall breakeven position across a three- year period (2023-26). The Scottish Government require NHS Boards to meet three key financial targets: ➢ Revenue Resource Limit (RRL) – a resource budget for ongoing activity; ➢ Capital Resource Limit (CRL) – a resource budget for net capital investment; and ➢ Cash Requirement – a financing requirement to fund the cash consequences of ongoing activity and net capital investment. Health Boards are required to contain their net expenditure within these limits, and report on any variation. NHS Grampian’s 2023/24 performance against these three key financial targets is shown in the table below: Statutory Financial Targets Limit as set Actual Variance by SGHSCD Outturn (Deficit)/Sur plus £000’s £000’s £000’s 1,440,632 1,440,535 97 Core Revenue Resource Limit 48,416 48,416 0 Non-Core Revenue Resource Limit 1,489,048 1,488,951 97 Total Revenue Resource Limit Core Capital Resource Limit 113,641 113,641 - Non-Core Capital Resource Limit 3,465 3,465 - Total Capital Resource Limit 117,106 117,106 - Cash Requirement 1,649,045 1,649,045 -

NHS Grampian was not able to set a balanced revenue budget for the 2023/24 financial year. The financial plan submitted to Scottish Government in March 2023 projected a £60.627 million deficit for the year after assuming savings achievement for the year of £16.557 million. Scottish Government confirmed additional funding for Health Boards in May which reduced the projected deficit to £42.936 million and further additional funding in February which has reduced the projected deficit to £25.103 million. The deficit highlights the level of financial challenge experienced during the year driven mainly by a combination of continuing service pressures and high levels of general inflation. Difficulties in recruitment and high demand for services have resulted in significant cost pressures in a number of areas, with the costs of supplementary staffing being a particular driver. Formal monthly reporting of the Board’s financial position to the Scottish Government commenced from quarter 1 with ongoing dialogue throughout the year relating to funding allocations and other shorter term cost control measures that could be implemented to mitigate the Boards financial position. In order to achieve in-year financial balance Scottish Government has provided the Board with financial support in the form of repayable brokerage of £24.800 million. The brokerage is repayable to the Scottish Government at the point in the future when the Board returns to financial balance. Without this additional support, the Board’s final outturn would have been an overspend of £24.703 million (equivalent to 1.66% of the Revenue Resource Limit). A surplus of £0.97 million against the Core Revenue Resource Limit is reported for 2023/24 reflecting this financial support. In November 2023, Scottish Government also informed NHS Grampian that the Board had been escalated from Stage 1 to Stage 2 of the NHS Scotland Support and Intervention Framework as a result of the Board’s financial position. This means that scheduled engagement and review of performance between the Scottish Government and the Board will continue through the usual channels. NHS Grampian submitted its 2024/25 Financial Plan to the Scottish Government in March 2024. The plan does not present a break-even position, recognising the significant work to

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS The Board expects to record a further revenue deficit in the 2024/25 financial year but is planning for the level of deficit to reduce over the following subsequent financial years as outlined in the Medium-Term Financial Plan. It recognises the significant work to be undertaken to bring the Board back into sustainable financial balance. The plan takes a risk-based approach recognising the need to identify and contain operational overspends without compromising on the level and quality of services NHS Grampian provides. Discussions will continue with the Scottish Government over the provision of further funding cover (known as “brokerage”) to support the projected revenue deficit. It is expected NHS Grampian will continue in operational existence for the foreseeable future, with no indication by Scottish Ministers of any intention for dissolution without transfer of services or functions to another entity with associated funding arrangements. 3.2.1 Delivering Sustainable Healthcare – Finding Balance This was the first time that NHS Grampian has overspent against its revenue budget and highlights the severe pressures that the health system faces in the North East of Scotland. A number of actions were taken to combat this overspend including the development in the year. These included amongst other measures: Development of an Integrated Performance Assurance Framework - Vacancy Controls - Review and control agency nursing and locum spend - Efficiency savings programme - Value and sustainability programme - 3.2.2 Efficiency Savings Efficiency savings of £17.52 million (2022/23 £10.2 million) were achieved against a target of £16.51 million (2022/23 £5.4 million) for NHS Grampian services. The scale of these savings was significantly higher than in the previous year and the delivery of these savings was supported by the development of an assurance and performance framework. A Programme Management Office was implemented to support this along with other elements of value and sustainability work. The main savings achieved were in the areas of agency spend including medical locum and nursing costs, hospital drug costs from switching from branded medicines to generic alternatives, procurement, cross boundary charges, energy efficiency and overtime costs.

The impact of those assets subject to an upward movement in valuation was an increase in the revaluation reserve of £48.457 million and assets subject to a downwards valuation seeing a reduction in the revaluation reserve of £4.032 million. Fraud, Bribery and Corruption NHS Grampian has a zero tolerance for fraud, bribery or corruption and has robust procedures in place, to reduce the likelihood of fraud occurring. These include an agreed policy on the Prevention, Detection and Investigation of Suspected Fraud, Theft and Corruption, Standing Financial Instructions incorporating Standards of Business Conduct and arrangements for the disclosure of information in the corporate register of interests, gifts, and hospitality. Staff are briefed regularly on counter fraud matters including the confidential routes that are available to report suspected fraud, bribery, or corruption. An awareness program was undertaken in the year focusing on promoting awareness on a number of areas as part of talent development for aspiring managers along with targeted development sessions to raise awareness on health sector fraud and general awareness for reporting concerns. NHS Grampian works closely with other organisations, including NHS Scotland Counter Fraud Services (CFS) and other government agencies to combat fraud. This includes participation in the bi-annual National Fraud Initiative which is a data matching exercise. The Board also takes part in a post payment patient exemption eligibility verification system, which covers all Family Health Service expenditure. 5. Payment Policy NHS Grampian is committed to supporting the Scottish Government in helping businesses during the current economic situation by paying bills more quickly. The intention is to achieve payment of all undisputed invoices within 10 working days, across all public bodies and NHS Grampian is actively working towards achievement of this accelerated payment target. - In 2023/24, the average credit taken was 14 days (2022/23 15 days). - In 2023/24 93% of invoices by value (2022/23 91%) and 90% of invoices by volume (2022/23 86%) were paid within 30 days. - In 2023/24 87% of invoices by value (2022/23 85%) and 79% of invoices by volume (2022/23 73%) were paid within 10 days. The small improvement in payment performance compared to the prior year reflects work to support the purchase to pay process during the year despite challenges around the impact of wider system pressures on support teams. 6. The Climate Change (Emissions Reductions Targets) (Scotland) Act 2019 amended this longer-term target to net-zero by 2045, five years in advance of the rest of the UK. In 2020, The Climate Change (Scotland) Amendment order came into force to reflect this and now requires NHS Boards to report on their progress in delivering their emissions reduction targets. NHS Scotland as a public body must reduce the greenhouse gas emissions from its activities, the activities under its control and from the electricity, steam and heat purchased by it to net-zero by 2040 or earlier where possible. All NHS owned buildings must be heated from renewable sources by 2038 or earlier where possible. The Board undertook a Climate Change Risk Assessment in 2023, whereby elements around the impact of climate change were assessed to look at the locked in impacts which climate change will have and the associated financial requirements to mitigate these factors. NHS Grampian’s Climate Emergency & Sustainability Strategy: Reimagining the Health Service for People & Planet 2023-2028 aligns to this and was approved by the NHS Grampian Board in October 2023 following extensive engagement through workshops, seminars, subject matter experts and informed discussion with external and internal stakeholders. The Framework of this strategy focuses on four main themes: ▪ Delivery of Net-Zero ▪ Greening Health Systems; ▪ Greening Places & Communities; and ▪ Collaboration across local and national systems. These set out our approach to respond to climate change adaptation, deliver environmental sustainability and promote biodiversity action as part of NHS Grampian’s Plan for the Future. NHS Grampian has a responsibility for the environmental stewardship of its extensive estate and facilities. Tackling climate change also presents an opportunity to improve the health and wellbeing of our communities, to avoid health harms and to reduce health

Role of the Board Grampian Health Board was established in 1972 under the National Health Services (Scotland) Act 1972 and is responsible for commissioning services for the residents of Grampian, a total population of over half a million people. NHS Boards working in partnership with Integration Joint Boards, form a local health system, with governing boards responsible for improving the health of their local populations and delivering the healthcare they require. The role of the Board is to: a) deliver patient centred, safe and effective health care to the population of Grampian; b) improve and protect the health of local people; c) improve health services for local people; d) reduce health inequalities; e) focus clearly on health outcomes and people’s experience of their local NHS system; f) promote integrated health and community planning by working closely with other local organisations; and g) provide a single focus of accountability for the performance of the local NHS system.

The charitable purpose of NHS Grampian Charity is to enhance healthcare and patient welfare in Grampian, through: a) improvement of the physical and mental health of the Grampian Health Board’s population and our staff; b) prevention, diagnosis and treatment of illness; c) provision of services and facilities in connection with above; and d) research into any matters relating to the causation, prevention, diagnosis or treatment of illness, or any other matters relating to the health service as the trustees see fit. Under the terms of the Public Bodies (Joint Working) (Scotland) Act 2014, three Integration Joint Boards (IJBs), Moray, Aberdeen City and Aberdeenshire, are established in Grampian with full delegation of functions and resources to enable integration of primary and community health and social care services. Executive and Non-Executive members of Grampian Health Board are appointed to represent the Board as voting members on each IJB. The voting membership, and therefore the exercise of control over each IJB, is shared equally between Grampian Health Board and the relevant Local Authority. 7. Strategic Vision, Themes and Values The Board’s strategic plan, Plan for the Future – 2022-28, was approved by the Board on 2 June 2022, following a widespread public consultation and engagement process. Board Assurance Framework The Grampian NHS Board is a board of governance which meets regularly throughout the year and has adopted a schedule of matters reserved for its decision. The overall role of the Board is to implement efficient, effective and accountable governance and to provide strategic leadership and direction for the system as a whole.

A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT The main functions of the Board are: ➢ strategy development and implementation; ➢ resource allocation; ➢ implementation of the Annual Delivery Plan; and ➢ performance management. In addition, as explained in the Directors’ Report on Page 38, all Board members are Trustees of the NHS Grampian Charity and are accountable in law for the discharge of the key duties of a charity Trustee as described in Section 66 of the Charities and Trustee Investment (“Scotland”) Act 2005. The Directors report on Page 38 also explains the establishment of the three Integration Joint Boards (IJBs), in Moray, Aberdeen City and Aberdeenshire. Under the terms of the Public Bodies (Joint Working) (Scotland) Act 2014 the Health Board and Local Authority delegate the responsibility for the strategic planning and delivery of adult health and social care services to each IJB. The delegation of services is governed by an integration scheme agreed by both partners and Executive and Non-Executive members of Grampian Health Board are appointed, to represent the Board, as voting members on each IJB. The voting membership, and therefore the exercise of control over each IJB is shared equally between Grampian Health Board and the relevant Local Authority. The Board’s performance management arrangements reflect those services delegated to the IJBs and the Chief Officers of each IJB attend each meeting of Grampian Health Board and each of the Board’s key Governance Committees as required. In support of this, the Board operates an assurance framework in accordance with the Blueprint for Good governance to provide active governance. Under the framework, all governance committees obtain assurance on behalf of the Board. This approach provides an agile and collaborative approach to governance of the Boards business and Board meetings receive approved minutes of committee minutes, as required under the Boards Standing Orders. A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT 4.3.2 The Grampian Operational Pressure Escalation System (G-OPES) The G-OPES model to escalate responses to demand pressures in the system continued to be used throughout 2023/24. G-OPES provides: A single approach to evaluate pressure across the health and care system A system view focusing on pathways of care supported by Portfolio leadership - Options to increase capacity through risk/pressure-based derogations of some - standards A whole system approach to share risk and offer internal mutual aid - Clarity for staff - Transparency - Specific protection of time-critical planned care - G-OPES includes at its highest levels a series of derogations to normal practice, which require decisions balancing three dimensions of quality of care against each other and using a risk and ethical based approach to reach a better overall position. Decisions about the derogations applied using G-OPES are taken by the system leadership. The pathways in G-OPES were reviewed in the year to ensure that they remain fit and aligned with Business Continuity and Service recovery plans to ensure responses to critical incidents or major incidents are managed through simple and user friendly command and control arrangements Regular daily or weekly meetings as appropriate confirm the G-OPES levels and the measures being applied will be recorded including the rationale for the decisions as outlined in the paper on the agenda. This includes escalate of more complex situations, or decisions where the risk lies outside the agreed tolerances to the Chief Executive Team. 5. Purpose of the System of Internal Control The system of internal control is based on an ongoing process designed to identify, prioritise and manage the principal risks facing the organisation. The system aims to evaluate the nature and extent of risks, and manage risks efficiently, effectively and economically. The system of internal control is designed to manage rather than eliminate the risk of failure to achieve the organisation’s aims and objectives. As such, it can only provide reasonable and not absolute assurance. The process within the organisation accords with guidance from Scottish Ministers in the SPFM and supplementary NHS guidance, and has been in place for the year up to the date of approval of the annual report and accounts. 6. Risk Management Arrangements All NHS Scotland bodies are subject to the requirements of the SPFM and must operate a risk management strategy in accordance with relevant guidance issued by the Scottish

A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT Ministers. The general principles for a successful risk management strategy are set out in the SPFM. Risk Management is a key element of the NHS Grampian Board’s internal controls and approach to governance. NHS Grampian’s Risk Management Policy outlines that all identified risks which have the potential to cause harm to patients, staff, contractors, the environment, our strategies, plans and objectives, should be managed in line with the NHSG Risk Management Protocol. Risks are proactively managed either at an operational, portfolio or executive level, with appropriate channels of escalation in place via governance structures. To support the success and effectiveness of NHS Grampian’s service delivery and governance arrangements, an enterprise risk management approach is adopted, which seeks to uniformly identify, assess and manage risk in a proactive manner. This approach is formalised within the NHS Grampian Risk Management Protocol. Enduring risks, which are risks that are ever-present within the system, are managed using controls and mitigations so that they present as low a threat as reasonably practical. The Risk Management Information System, Datix, is utilised to document and manage risk, within Risk Registers. All risks are rated using the NHS Scotland Risk Matrices. NHS Grampian’s Strategic Risk Register aims to identify and articulate significant risk that has the potential to impact upon the achievement of the organisation’s strategic objectives, as outlined in Plan for the Future. The effective identification and management of the Board’s Strategic Risks aids assurance that activities and the prioritisation of resources are aligned with the achievement of operational priorities and strategic objectives. During 2023/2024 an enhanced process of oversight, accountability and management of Strategic Risk commenced. The commencement of these activities has seen the further development of several risks, including re-framing to bring in line with the current operating environment. Although it should be noted that the material essence of the risks remain unchanged from the previous year. Within this process, the Chief Executive Team are accountable for the development and management of Strategic Risk, performing regular review activities at their Performance and Business meetings. Board Committees scrutinise deep dive reports presented for their aligned risks, assessing the efficacy of control measures and mitigations, and the level of assurance that can be provided for the management of the risks. The Audit and Risk Committee have responsibility and a key focus during 2023/2024 has been improving the formal recording of Action Planning relating to risks documented within the organisation’s risk registers. The Audit and Risk Committee have responsibility for the scrutiny of the Strategic Risk Register in its entirety, and for oversight for monitoring the effectiveness of arrangements for the NHS Grampian Board’s systems and processes for risk management. 6.1 Risk Appetite

During 2023/2024 the new process of oversight, accountability and management of Strategic Risk commenced, aimed at improving the control environment within this area. The Chief Executive Team have accountability for the development and management of Strategic Risk within NHS Grampian. To assist in the management of these risks, regular formal review activities are undertaken during CET Performance or Business Meetings. During reviews, individual risks are considered against the entire Strategic Risk profile and against the Board’s Risk Appetite. The format and cycle of reporting and review supports the robust assessment of Strategic Risk and efficacy of control measures and mitigations, from a system wide perspective. Any gaps in controls or assurances during review are highlighted and actions identified. Strategic Risks are aligned to Board Committees in accordance with their remit and Terms of Reference. The Board Committees conduct an overseeing role, which encompasses scrutiny regarding the management of their aligned risk(s), including associated controls, mitigations, performance and related issues. The Committee is provided with the Strategic Risk Register at each meeting and any assurance notes regarding individual Strategic Risk scrutiny at Other Board Committee meetings. The next notification of all SNAP audits will be for 2023 and will be reported in next year’s update. ➢ Healthcare Improvement Scotland (HIS) o To 31 March 2024, there has been one HIS Safe Delivery of Care inspection reports published in relation to NHS Grampian. Inspection of Aberdeen Royal Infirmary (ARI) and Dr Gray’s Hospital (DGH) was conduct in October 2023. The reports published on 1 February 2024 had 32 requirements (20 DGH, 12 ARI), 3 recommendations (1 DGH, 2 ARI) and 7 areas of good practice (2 DGH, 5 ARI). Work is ongoing on the actions to address the findings, with the 18-week Improvement Action Plan follow up to be submitted to HIS on the week commencing 3 June 2024. o An unannounced Infection Prevention and Control Inspection of Mental Health Services in Royal Cornhill Hospital & Great Western Lodge was conducted on the 28th & 29th March 2023. The report was published on Thursday 15th June 2023. There were 8 requirements, 1 recommendation and 4 areas of good practice, with the 18-week Improvement Action Plan follow up was submitted on 2nd November 2023 o All HIS reviews/inspection reports published are reviewed organisationally and NHS Grampian have developed a process for communicating and sharing HIS Inspection reports and associated learning. This process ensures widespread information sharing and associated recommendations to engage and prepare stakeholders for upcoming visits. ➢ Mental Welfare Commission (MWC) The MWC has published 14 visits to NHS Grampian up to 31 March 2024, 7 have been “announced” visits, and 7 have been “unannounced”. There have been 9 visits to Royal Cornhill Hospital and one visit to Great Western Lodge, one visit to Seafield Hospital, one visit to Fraserburgh Hospital, and one visit to Dr Gray’s Hospital. ➢ Work continues towards the development of maternity services at Dr Gray’s

A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT at following link: https://www.gov.scot/publications/report-moray-maternity-services- review/ and the Scottish Government ambition described in ‘The Best Start: A Five Year Forward Plan for Maternity and Neonatal Care in Scotland (2017)’. The ambition of this work continues to be the provision of safe, deliverable, sustainable, and high-quality maternity services for the women and families of Moray. Work has also commenced on planning towards the implementation of a new model of neonatal care in Scotland in line with the recommendations in ‘The Best Start: A Five Year Forward Plan for Maternity and Neonatal Care in Scotland (2017)’. The new model will see Scotland moving to a networked model of three neonatal intensive care units (NICU) working alongside local neonatal units (LNU) and special care units (SCU), supported by transitional care and community care services. Aberdeen will be one of the three neonatal intensive care units. ( Maternity and neonatal services - neonatal intensive care plan: options appraisal - gov.scot (www.gov.scot )) 7.3 Infrastructure ➢ Enhanced monitoring arrangements remain in place for our capital and infrastructure projects to address the impact on design, programme of market forces in the construction industry, transport and supply chain issues and more recently geopolitical events including armed conflict. ➢ Governance arrangements for our major capital projects now include the requirement for an independent external design review following recent learning from other large health sector projects. ➢ Major asset under construction projects have been subject to enhanced reporting arrangement. ➢ Arrangements are in place to ensure that available resource is allocated based on assessed risk criteria and with due regard to deliverability. ➢ The Performance, Assurance, Finance and Infrastructure Committee receive an annual update on progress against backlog maintenance risks. 7.4 Innovation and Transformation The board launched a revised digital strategy ‘Service Transformation through Digital: a strategy 2023 – 2027” in December 2023. The strategy set out the intended approach by NHS Grampian in regards to the delivery of digital solutions to support modernisation of service delivery. The strategy aim was to improve health outcomes and improve citizens experiences through the better use and access to information and help drive financial sustainability.

A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT The strategy will be achieved by developing systems, processes and digital solutions that support staff and citizens having secure access to the information they need, when and where they need to. The strategy is aligned with NHS Grampians Plan for the Future and Scottish Government Digital strategy. Further work to ensure that the Strategy is aligned to new financial and national delivery positions, has started and will continue in next financial year. The board undertook the following innovation and transformation measures in the year in support of this strategy: ▪ Implementation of in-patient multi-professional key documents. The work program in 2023/24 focused on the migration from legacy systems. This is migration will provide a standardised electronic patient record and improvement of waiting list management for Mental Health and Learning Disabilities. The complexity and format of the work across multiple facilities will be a multi-year business activity. ▪ Implementation of the Hospital Electronic Prescribing and Medicines Administration (HEPMA) system in Hospitals across NHS Grampian took place throughout the year. HEPMA replaced the paper based systems in hospitals across the North East of Scotland. The system provides a range of clinical, safety, informatics and management benefits to prescribing and administration of medicines, which would not otherwise be achieved using our current paper based systems improving patient safety and experience. 7.5 Workforce Triangulation of data provided by Scottish Government’s Financial Delivery Unit with local workforce information indicates that by December 2023 substantive workforce costs as a proportion of revenue expenditure in NHS Grampian have increased to 61.5% from 57.4% in 2019. This 4.1% increase equates to approximately £55m in 2023/24 revenue budget terms. This upward pressure can be put down to a number of factors, including a larger whole time equivalent workforce and pay inflation, although the latter has to date largely been offset by increases in baseline funding. In the context of the very significant financial pressures the Board faces, and given the limited levers available to reduce the size and cost of the workforce because of longstanding national policy commitments and a lack of available funding for schemes such as voluntary severance or early retirement, continued focussed work on local sustainability and value programme objectives relating to workforce costs is vital. These include: controls on agency and supplementary staffing use; reducing the number of non-compliant rotas for Doctors and Dentists in Training (DDiTs); reductions in overtime expenditure; and enhanced vacancy controls. In accordance with national policy direction, and local objectives and KPIs for

A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT management we have made significant progress in reducing the use of Agency and supplementary nurse staffing spend. This has been balanced with the need to ensure that safe levels of staffing are maintained, particularly in relation to our ability to meet demand pressures in urgent, unscheduled and planned care, which assessment of the Board’s aligned strategic risk indicates are at intolerable levels. Progress has also been made in respect of reducing the use of Agency medical staff, although the targeted financial savings have not been realised to such an extent. We have contributed to the national work on sustainability and value in supplementary medical staffing led by the Chief Medical Officer’s Directorate in Scottish Government. Local work commenced during 2022/23 on increasing the Direct Engagement of medical locums has been supported by re-apportioning some of the lower than required funding available to develop a Medical Bank whilst a re- scoping of the requirements is undertaken by medical management with support from workforce colleagues. This will draw on learning from participation in the above national work. Work has also been progressed in 2023/24 in an attempt to reverse the increasing number of Junior Doctor rotas that are, through routine monitoring, deemed to be non-compliant. Not only does this have a financial implication for the organisation, but also the health and wellbeing of DDiTs because it is likely to reflect breaks not being taken as they should be, rather than a shortage of staff. External benchmarking with other Boards has identified steps through greater clarity on and consistency in the management and supervision of DDiTs that it is hoped can address this issue. A re-monitoring exercise is planned for early 2024/25. Initial work on the local sustainability and value project to reduce overtime indicates that throughout the majority of 2023/24 there has been a reduction in both overtime and excess hours. However, the net benefit of this in terms of the Board’s financial position is felt likely to be cost avoidance rather than a significant reduction in revenue spending, given the increased hourly rates payable across all staff groups from 2023/24, with provision for overtime not included in baseline funding uplifts from Scottish Government. Given the recent confirmation by the Cabinet Secretary for Health and Social Care of a reduction in the working week for Agenda for Change staff with effect from 1 st April 2024, and the enactment of the Health and Care (Staffing) (Scotland) Act 2019 on the same date, these controls are likely to come under greater pressure moving forward as the unit costs of employment increase. Given that current indications are that national funding provided in respect of the reforms will not fully meet the cost of implementation, careful monitoring of the impact of the controls described above will be required, alongside work to redesign services and re-shape the workforce. Accordingly, work has commenced in partnership on a framework for services to use in pursuing the aims of NHS Grampian’s Plan for the Future and the need to work within a more limited financial envelope, whilst keeping clinical, financial and staff governance, and a focus on prevention in balance.

A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT Key to this, given the limited levers available for re-shaping the workforce, will be continued operation of the enhanced vacancy management controls introduced in December 2023. Early indications are that whilst a significant proportion of the vacancies submitted to the scrutiny process since then have been approved as meeting the agreed criteria for filling posts, when year on year data is compared for the first quarter of 2023 and 2024, the controls may have supported a 30% reduction in requests to recruit. This will require further review of data through 2024/25. However, this suggests that the net saving from this quarter alone may exceed the linked annual savings target, if alternative methods of securing additional resource to cover the capacity lost as a result are not used by services. The staffing report on Page 77 provides more detail on the various recruitment initiatives underway. 7.6 Compliance ➢ A Compliance Group operates to provide cross system focus and monitoring on compliance against regulatory and statutory requirements. These requirements include amongst others, GDPR and Data Performance targets, health and safety requirements, compliance against NHS Standards and other areas of compliance. The group provides flash reports and bi annual reports which were received throughout the year on the work of the Compliance Sub-Group from the Director of Infrastructure and Sustainability providing assurance to the Senior Leadership team and the Audit and Risk Committee. The group is responsible for providing: ▪ A forum on a system wide engagement around compliance matters deemed “High” or “Very High” risk to NHS Grampian. This is a continuously evolving area involving a comprehensive mix of attendees and subject matter experts. ▪ A risk control model for the oversight of ‘High’ and ‘Very Risks’ for compliance related areas and monitoring arrangements through exception reporting and periodic reports to the Compliance Group. ▪ Where an area of non-compliance is identified, support the development of an appropriate action plan and follow up and monitoring of its progress. ▪ Periodic review of its role and remit to ensure that emerging or new compliance risks are identified and captured as part of monitoring arrangements. ➢ An Information Security group is in place to co-ordinate all cyber security matters including those relating to medical devices. The group has links to all aspects of the eHealth governance structure including the Information

A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT Group and Civil Contingencies Group and is responsible for the following actions: ▪ Review existing cyber security policies and procedures, including access management controls, threat and vulnerability and patch management, to ensure that the arrangements for cyber security of medical devices, including roles and responsibilities, are appropriately reflected in extant guidance for the service. ▪ Procedures to ensure the periodic review of cyber security risks, including those identified for medical devices, reported in the Board’s risk management system (Datix), and that, where appropriate, this information is reconciled to equipment asset registers. ▪ Working closely with key stakeholders such as Information Security, Information Governance and Procurement teams, develop local guidance for the incorporation of Information Security requirements within the procurement specification and tender process. ▪ Periodic penetration testing of medical device security. ➢ A process is in place and administered by the Board’s Medical Equipment Management Service to ensure that all medical equipment assets across NHS Grampian are recorded on the Board’s equipment asset register, including relevant cyber security information. ➢ Good progress has been made against action plan around NHS Grampian’s Information governance being put in place following the Information Commissioner’s Office audit in 2022/23. The action plan now forms part of the Information Governance Strategy and Improvement Plan 2023 – 2025 with regular progress updates provided to the Compliance Sub-group, Clinical Risk meeting and Information Governance Steering Group. 7.7 Involvement and Engagement ➢ The Board continue to progress agreement on a consistent vision and values aimed at ensuring a consistent message in all communication between clinical staff, service users and their carers. The Board’s Assurance Framework recognises the role of staff side and the advisory structure in supporting the work of Board sub-committees. ➢ The Population Health Committee adopted the Fairer Scotland Duty principles as part of its business including an Integrated Impact Assessment which ensures that appropriate consideration has been given in the development of services and policies by Service Leads through the lens of equalities and inequalities. This was supported by the Engagement and Empowerment Oversight sub-group with an Engagement and Empowerment Plan aligned to the Plan for the Future. Progress reports distributed to the wider system

In support of this a joint review was undertaken around complaints handing for the IJBs and NHS Grampian Health Board to provide assurance around cross system handling of complaints. 8. Best Value I can confirm that Grampian Health Board is committed to ensuring that its activities are undertaken in a manner that will secure best value in the use of public funds in line with the arrangements set out in the Scottish Public Finance Manual. The Board incorporates the principles of best value within its planning, performance and delivery activities ensuring that they are part of everyday business and integral to the Board’s decision making in all key areas. In addition, the Board continues to seek opportunities to enhance the system of internal control with a specific focus on the delivery of safe and effective patient care, achievement of priority access targets and demonstrating best value and the efficient use of resources. 9. Review of Adequacy and Effectiveness As Accountable Officer, I am responsible for reviewing the adequacy and effectiveness of the system of internal control. My review is informed by: - Executive and senior managers who are required to develop, implement and maintain adequate internal controls across their areas of responsibility; - The work of the internal auditor, who submits regular reports to the Audit and Risk Committee which include their independent and objective opinion on the effectiveness of risk management, internal control and governance processes; - Management and other reports issued by the Board’s external auditor and the external auditors of the NHS Grampian Endowment Funds; - Financial plans, service plans and related organisational performance and risk management reports presented to the Board and relevant governance committees;

A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT - Transparent reporting and analysis of the Board’s financial performance through the Performance Assurance, Finance & Infrastructure Committee including the extent to which the Board is reliant on non-recurring sources of funding to achieve our financial targets; and - An annual briefing to the Performance, Assurance, Finance, and Infrastructure Committee on progress against the backlog maintenance implementation plan aimed at reducing high risk backlog maintenance in clinical areas and compliance with climate change legislation. In addition to the above, the processes that have been applied to assist me in reviewing the effectiveness of the system of internal control include: - Annual statements of assurance from each of the core governance committees of the Board, including the NHS Grampian Charity Committee, with respect to the governance arrangements that exist for the NHS Grampian Charity Funds charity which is consolidated with the main Board accounts; - Written confirmation from executive and senior managers that controls within their individual areas of responsibility are adequate and have been operating effectively throughout the year; - Assurance from the External Auditor of the NHS Grampian Charity Funds, in their management letter, that expenditure complies with the charitable purpose and that charity Funds have not been used retrospectively for expenditure originally authorised as a commitment against exchequer funds; - Independent consideration of the Governance Statement and its disclosures by Internal Audit and the Audit and Risk Committee; - Consideration and approval of the Annual Accounts, including the Governance Statement by the Board; and - During the year, minutes of the meetings of the core governance committees were provided to all Board members. I have been advised on the implications of the result of my review of the effectiveness of the system of internal control by the Board and the Audit and Risk Committee and a plan to address weaknesses and ensure continuous improvement of the system is in place. 10. Significant Issues As Accountable Officer, I am required to record issues that are significant enough to warrant recording: Funding arrangement whilst delivering on clinical and people including workforce and citizens governance and maintaining a focus on prevention present a significant issue.

A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT NHS Grampian submitted its 2024/2025 financial plan to the Scottish Government in March 2024. The Board expects to record a further revenue deficit in the 2024/25 financial year but is planning for the level of deficit to reduce over the following subsequent financial years as outlined in the Medium Term Financial Plan. Specific financial risks forecast to materialise include implementation of the non-pay elements of the 2023 pay deal and the financial performance on the IJB given challenging operating environment. Significant work to be undertaken to bring the Board back into sustainable financial balance. Discussions will continue with the Scottish Government over the provision of further funding cover (known as “brokerage”) to support the projected revenue deficit. 11. Disclosures At the date of issue on 28 June 2024 based on the evidence considered during my review of the effectiveness of the internal control environment operating within NHS Grampian and group during 2023/24, I am not aware of any outstanding significant control weaknesses or other failures to achieve the standards set out in the guidance on governance, risk management and control.

THE REMUNERATION REPORT Remuneration of Executive and Non-Executive Members The total remuneration disclosed on Pages 73 to 76 includes all amounts paid or payable by the Board in each financial year and a separate assessment of the estimated increase in the cumulative pension benefit that will be payable following retirement. Board Members’ Contracts of Employment The Executive Board members of Grampian Health Board are employed on permanent contracts of employment which require a minimum of three months’ notice by either party to terminate the contract. The Non-Executive members are ministerial appointments on contracts of between two and four years. The terms and conditions of Executive and Senior Management Cohort and Non- Executive Members including annual remuneration, and any entitlement to severance pay, is determined by the Scottish Government under Ministerial Direction and in accordance with PCS(ESM) /3. As of 1 April 2023 Executive and Senior Managers returned to standard terms and conditions after a period of three years of a variation to terms and conditions of being able to be offered overtime payments in line with paragraph 3.1 of the Agenda for Change Handbook during the COVID-19 outbreak. Assessment of Performance The performance assessment process for the Executive and Senior Management Cohort is in accordance with PCS (ESM) 2019/1. Performance management and appraisal arrangements follow the nationally prescribed format and are directly linked to the improved performance of Grampian Health Board in the delivery of its objectives. Written evidence relating to the performance ratings for Executive members, following review by the Chief Executive and Chair, is made available to the Remuneration Committee for approval. For the Chief Executive, the process is undertaken by the Chair.

B REMUNERATION AND STAFF REPORT For Non-Executive members, the process is also undertaken by the Chair and written evidence is submitted to the Cabinet Secretary for Health and Wellbeing. The progression of the Executive and Senior Management Cohort through the pay range is subject to, as a minimum, the fully acceptable performance of the individual. A National Performance Management Committee as detailed in PCS (ESM) 2019/1 ensures, on behalf of the Scottish Government, the effective and consistent application of pay and performance management arrangements for NHS Scotland Executives subject to Ministerial Direction. Remuneration Committee The Remuneration Committee ensures that arrangements are in place to comply with NHS Grampian policy and Scottish Government direction and guidance for determining the employment, remuneration and terms and conditions of employment and for approving the personal objectives of all Executive Directors and Senior Managers. Further information including membership of the Remuneration Committee, is included in the Governance Statement on Page 47 above.
